symbols: outstretched arms on the cross — Christ's embrace of all humanity in His sacrifice
metallic wings — divine nature and heavenly glory manifested even in the moment of death
ivory corpus — the purity and beauty of the sinless victim
cross as axis — the meeting point of heaven and earth, vertical and horizontal
head bowed — Christ's death, 'He bowed His head and gave up His spirit'
description: A sculptural crucifix featuring a white ivory or pale corpus of Christ mounted on a dark cross. Behind the figure, large metallic silver-gray wings fan out symmetrically, creating a dramatic halo-like effect. Christ's head is tilted to one side, eyes closed, wearing a loincloth. The wings are composed of many radiating metal feathers or rays. The background is dark red or maroon fabric. The overall effect is striking and luminous.
theological_description: This unusual crucifix combines the imagery of death and glory by placing wings behind the crucified Christ. The wings may suggest several theological readings: Christ as the 'Sun of Righteousness' rising with healing in His wings (Malachi 4:2); the divine nature that cannot be extinguished even in death; or the protective wings of God under which the faithful find refuge (Psalm 91:4). The combination subverts the shame of crucifixion by revealing the glory hidden within it. Paul writes that Christ 'disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them in the cross' (Colossians 2:15). What appears to be defeat is actually victory; what looks like death is the birthplace of eternal life. The wings transform the instrument of execution into a throne of glory.

scripture_refs.reason: He bowed His head and gave up His spirit — the moment of death depicted in the bowed head
The Sun of Righteousness shall rise with healing in His wings — the wings behind the crucified Christ
Triumphing over powers and authorities through the cross — the glory manifest in crucifixion
Under His wings you will find refuge — the protective divine presence even at the cross
